ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pho with beef tendon and tripe. FOODON_00002700 (beef soup food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Beef soup) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for pho with tendon and tripe in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese beef noodle soup preparation.

allergens: gluten (possible cross-contamination from soy sauce or condiments)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef tendon and tripe pho weighing approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, sliced beef tendon, tripe, and garnishes such as bean sprouts, fresh herbs, and chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; beef tendon and tripe values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB entries for cooked beef tendon and cooked beef tripe
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for pho with beef tendon and tripe as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for: (1) cooked beef tendon (NDB 13352 / FDC 174033), (2) cooked beef tripe (FDC 172543), (3) cooked rice noodles (FDC 168871), and (4) beef bone broth, weighted to a representative bowl composition of approximately 30% broth, 25% rice noodles, 20% beef tendon, 15% beef tripe, and 10% garnishes. Per-100g values represent the blended dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed beef soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pho with beef tendon and tripe. GI estimated at 40 based on: (1) rice noodles (cooked) GI ≈ 40–53 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); (2) the substantial protein and fat content from beef tendon and tripe, and the high water content of the broth, which attenuate glycemic response relative to plain noodles; (3) analogous mixed beef noodle soup entries in the Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is a conservative low estimate. GL per serving = (40 × 39.0 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 15.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit condiment additions, and avoid drinking the soup broth entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from tendon and tripe
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 15.6 per bowl is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of rice noodles, consider requesting a smaller noodle portion, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef offal and bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~2100 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~3.5 g per bowl) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, avoid drinking the full broth, and choose leaner protein accompaniments where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Tendon and Tripe Pho (Phở bò gân và sách) is a Vietnamese noodle soup dish featuring slow-simmered beef bone broth infused with aromatic spices including star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and charred ginger and onion, served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) with sliced or whole pieces of braised beef tendon and honeycomb beef tripe. Beef tendon is a collagen-rich connective tissue that becomes gelatinous and tender after prolonged simmering, contributing a silky mouthfeel and body to the broth. Beef tripe, the stomach lining of cattle, has a chewy, slightly firm texture and a mild, savoury flavour that absorbs the spiced broth well. The dish is typically garnished with fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime wedges, sliced chilli, and hoisin or sriracha sauce on the side. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~360 kcal per bowl), relatively high in protein from the offal components (~31 g per bowl), and moderate in carbohydrate from the rice noodles (~39 g per bowl). The primary clinical concern is sodium: the bone broth and fish sauce contribute approximately 2100 mg sodium per bowl, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (~15.6 per bowl), making portion control of noodles advisable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Beef tendon is notably rich in collagen and glycine. Tripe provides vitamin B12, zinc, and iron. This dish is served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore and is popular as a hearty, warming meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef tendon pho?
Is beef tripe pho high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for pho with beef tendon and tripe as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked beef tendon, cooked beef tripe, cooked rice noodles, and beef bone broth, weighted by a representative bowl composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is set to 0.0 as beef tendon and tripe contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant sources are below the reporting threshold in the source data (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries). The dish is sold at SGD 17.90 at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt - Circular Road on field survey data. Halal status is listed as false as this cannot be confirmed from the available source data; the restaurant should be verified independently. The cuisine field is set to Vietnamese as this is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore; the input record listed cuisine as Singapore which reflects the serving location rather than the dish origin. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual serving sizes may vary. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimate.
